title: What Would the Bigger House Actually Fix?
subtitle: A journal practice for separating real housing needs from what you are asking the house to carry
excerpt: A journal practice for examining the gap between the home you have and the one you picture. Asks which parts of the dissatisfaction are about the house and which parts the house cannot fix, wherever you live.

content.text: A twenty-minute journal practice for the home dissatisfaction loop. Not to talk yourself out of wanting more, but to separate the real housing needs from what you are asking the house to carry on your behalf.
Describe the home you picture in detail. What does it have that yours doesn't?
Write the specifics, not the feeling. More space, a bigger kitchen, a garden, a study. Get the list onto paper so you can look at it.
What feeling do you associate with that home? What do you imagine you would feel like living there?
Write the feeling as precisely as you can: calm, ordered, proud, safe, free. One word or a phrase.
Of the practical things the bigger house would fix, which are real practical problems?
Not enough bedrooms for the people who live there, no space to work from home, nowhere for the children to play outside. These are real. Write them separately from the rest.
Which of the feelings you listed could exist in the home you have now, with some changes?
Not all of them, necessarily. But some. Write what would need to shift inside the current home for that feeling to have more room.
Is there anything you are asking the house to fix that the house cannot fix?

The desire for a bigger house sometimes carries other desires inside it: more time, less stress, a different chapter, a version of your life that feels more settled. Write what those might be, if any come up.
